report.cost_of_living: Tokyo has a high cost of living, especially rent, which can take up a large share of a graduate student's stipend. Groceries and dining out are moderate, and many students find scholarships or part-time work necessary to stay comfortable.
report.academic: Computer Science graduate programs in Tokyo are rigorous, with a strong emphasis on research output and lab culture. Advisor relationships are important and can vary widely; workload is heavy but manageable with good time management.
report.visa: Student visas are straightforward to obtain but require renewal and careful paperwork. Transitioning to a work visa after graduation is possible but involves navigating a fairly bureaucratic process.
report.culture_shock: Workplace and academic culture tends to be conservative and hierarchy-conscious. Language barriers are real for daily life even though many CS programs operate in English; newcomers need time to adapt to social norms and etiquette.
report.community: Tokyo has a sizable international student community and several international-student support offices on campus. Finding community is easier within university circles than in the broader city.
report.career: Job prospects for CS graduates are strong, both locally at Japanese tech firms and multinational companies, and internationally given the reputation of Japanese engineering training.
report.safety: Tokyo is widely regarded as one of the safest large cities in the world, with low crime rates even late at night.
report.climate: Humid summers and mild winters; typhoon season in late summer can disrupt routines but is rarely dangerous if precautions are taken.
report.part_time_work: International students can legally work part-time up to 28 hours per week with proper permission, which helps offset living costs but adds pressure on an already heavy academic schedule.
